author: @Haplo-san
text: Once I was seen and also tried myself a weird bicycle a neighbour's kid had 25+ years ago. When you were pedalling forward, it was working as intended. But if you try to pedal backwards, it was locking pedals and doing rear brake, so hard so I could have lock the rear wheel and drift like a champion with a bit of bicycling skills I had. I was loved it because first, it was different than every bicycle I know of and I couldn't have understand the mysterious mechanism. And second, my rim brakes wasn't locking the wheels hard as I wanted always, probably lack of maintenance and overused worn brakes. Now seeing this, I think it had a thing like this to brake.

author: @oscarvogel2140
text: Alternator pullys on 2000+ VWs have a one way bearing. So the alternator momentum doesn't push the crank sprocket when slowing down RPMs. This could cause the crank bolt to loosen. They are small diameter and run on a serpentine belt.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: select the items you want to centre by either holding down the shift key and left mouse clicking them or drag a selection box around the items - the pull down shape menu tells you the number of items you have selected - when you have selected them click the align icon then click on the item you want to align to and you will see the align buttons come up - hope this helps

author: @justtinkering6713
text: A company back in the 70's came up with a material that would do the ratchet and pawl function. The company was 3M, and the material was called "Fibertran". It consisted of two mating surfaces that had hairs on them that lay at an angle. They would slip past each other in one direction and grab each other in the other direction. It was a kind of a directional Velcro.

Unfortunately you have neglected  the other giggly bits.  Please.  Please  on behalf of a  Irish  herion addict of over 20 years  .who is  now drug free over 3 years  thanks to you and other wonderful people like mick grewcock .please take the time to appreciate yourself as much as we appreciate you.  I said it ages ago  . I say it right now. People like you leave this world a better place than when they found it.   I shall try to right the negative affects I  have created.  Thank you Robert and Luke  for not only teaching me new skills but also teaching me how to be a productive member of society.  I write this  having consumed a few beers today but having resisted drugs for over 3 years.  I shall continue my endeavour until I have exhausted my supply of good intentions. I shall try to contribute in a positive way as I can.

author: @philip5940
text: You love that 3d printer . Do the plastic parts produced have much durability ?

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text:
It's surprisingly durable if your printer is 1:properly set up. 2: you choose the correct filament plastic for the application. 3: your slicer settings are good. 

If either one of these things go wrong,  you could end up with a weak or bad product. If it goes right which it usually does, then it can be strong enough to be used as weaponry.  Lots of 3D printed firearms in USA, here in Europe we can't have that, so I just got myself a 3D printed crossbow instead:p works like a charm.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: @DKFX1 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they are pretty strong and there are ways to make them even stronger!

author: @401ksolar
text: Back in the 1960s we put together the Hub with a slip clutch from a bicycle and a cable running to a buoy in a nearby lake, it didn't generate much but as the waves came in it did keep the flywheel going, it's really neat seeing all the projects that you're taking to the next level that we did as kids back in the sixties LOL

replies.author: @williamchamberlain2263
replies.text: It'd be interesting to see how well that coped with fouling of the cable, because the rest of it sounds less vulnerable to fouling and debris than most other wave- or sea-power designs.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @williamchamberlain2263 well it was a local Constable that put an end to our project LOL, they did not want an anchor Point screwed into the lake bed so for a long-term result I have no idea we were just a bunch of kids playing with ideas

@ThinkingandTinkering Very first test had some success.. it doesn't roll the easiest in the right direction, but it sure doesn't go the other way. Tolerances need slight adjustment though.
Using 20 rollers 1cm long, cut from 1.25mm diameter wire. And I've figured out the arrangement for a functional mechanism, but have a bit more to do to finish that.

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, strictly speaking you are right 👍. Most use "sprag" and "one-way" interchangeably. Yours is a roller clutch type one way bearing. Probably best suited to your application, less drag 😁.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @tradermunky1998
text:
Same thing happened here in the US, everyone hated seat belts. As a kid, we had a station wagon and all the kids would be jumping between the backseat and back part of the wagon 🤣

Then they made it law in the front seats. Then it became law for 14 and younger in the backseat. Then everyone, both front and back. There are even special laws now depending on the height of the child and seat type!

Be (ultra) safe everyone 😜

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we do too - to my mind it has got a bit crazy

author: @Dennis_M1DLG
text: Seatbelts are designed to stretch 11mm for every 1M of webbing during a crash. If you have had a sudden breaking situation, or do not know the history of a seatbelt (eg you buy a second hand car)  replace it, the webbing only absorbs impact once-over and a second crash or hard breaking incident will result in less protection.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Interesting.  Having a little play can also increase the life of some devices and designs.

replies.author: @Dennis_M1DLG
@jb-dn3ct
replies.text: That is very good to know, thank you for sharing this knowledge 👍
